[2026-07-03 18:13:00 INFO] flask app.py did not start for online log
[2026-07-03 18:13:00 INFO] Team leader: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a Knowledge Graph Advisor. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your job is to review process models and provide actionable suggestions for improvement, one at a time.
You use extra knowledge provided to you to inform your suggestions. Your main responsibilities include giving suggestions and your reasoning to the process design expert.
Here is a new customer's task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider.]

Current process text:
<process>
  <activity role='Ontario hospitals' action='develop risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF)' objects='risk-stratification tools' id='activity1'/>
  <activity role='Ontario hospitals' action='consistently apply risk-stratification tools across all Ontario hospitals' objects='risk-stratification tools' id='activity2_1'/>
  <activity role='Ontario hospitals' action='use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department' objects='standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process' id='activity2_2'/>

  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='perform risk assessment/stratification' objects='standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process' id='activity3'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch2'>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
        <branch id='branch3'>
          <activity role='clinicians' action='order and collect bloodwork panel (serum creatinine, electrolytes, troponin, complete blood count)' objects='bloodwork specimens and lab orders' id='activity4b'/>
          <activity role='healthcare expert' action='interpret bloodwork results and communicate clinically significant findings' objects='bloodwork results' id='activity4c'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch6'>
          <activity role='clinicians' action='perform electrocardiogram' objects='electrocardiogram' id='activity7'/>
          <activity role='healthcare expert' action='interpret electrocardiogram and communicate clinically significant findings' objects='electrocardiogram result' id='activity7b'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch7'>
          <activity role='clinicians' action='perform chest x-ray examination' objects='chest x-ray examination' id='activity8'/>
          <activity role='healthcare expert' action='interpret chest x-ray and communicate clinically significant findings' objects='chest x-ray result' id='activity8b'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch8'>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
            <branch condition='no recent echocardiogram is available' id='branch9'>
              <activity role='clinicians' action='perform an echocardiogram' objects='echocardiogram' id='activity9'/>
              <activity role='healthcare expert' action='interpret echocardiogram and communicate clinically significant findings' objects='echocardiogram result' id='activity9b'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='a recent echocardiogram is available' id='branch10'>
              <activity role='clinicians' action='proceed without an echocardiogram' objects='' id='activity10'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch11'>
          <activity role='clinicians' action='frequently measure heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized' objects='heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation' id='activity11'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>

  <activity role='clinicians' action='calculate an acute heart failure risk score (e.g., the EHMRG)' objects='EHMRG risk score' id='activity12'/>

  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
    <branch condition='low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) AND responded to initial treatment in the ED AND no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.)' id='branch12'>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='discharge home without requiring inpatient admission' objects='patient' id='activity13'/>
      <activity role='primary care provider' action='provide a follow-up visit within days of discharge from the ED' objects='follow-up visit' id='activity14'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5)' id='branch13'>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='admit to hospital' objects='patient' id='activity15'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='average intensity' id='branch14'>
          <activity role='inpatient care' action='provide inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing' objects='inpatient care' id='activity16'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='high intensity' id='branch15'>
          <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
            <branch condition='requires ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation)' id='branch21'>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
                <branch condition='requires ventilation (noninvasive)' id='branch16'>
                  <activity role='clinicians' action='provide noninvasive ventilation' objects='ventilation' id='activity17'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='requires ventilation (invasive)' id='branch17'>
                  <activity role='clinicians' action='provide invasive ventilation' objects='ventilation' id='activity18'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='requires admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing' id='branch18'>
              <activity role='intensive care unit' action='admit to intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing' objects='intensive care unit admission' id='activity19'/>
            </branch>
          </inclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>

  <activity role='Ontario hospitals' action='have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider' objects='pathway or mechanism' id='activity21'/>
</process>
Extra knowledge:
1) The activity 'Provide cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies...' should be the last activity after everything else is done and before the end activity.
2) Activities related to stroke units, staffing, or hospitals in the QBP should be prioritized as the initial steps in their respective flow or branch as they are prerequisites for other activities.
3) All tests, including any type of imaging and blood tests, should be immediately interpreted (followed) by a healthcare expert.
4) Blood tests or any tests that take some time to get results should occur concurrently with other activities to allow faster treatment unless the test results are required for a specific task, in which case they will be merged into the flow or branch of that activity as they are now prerequisites.
5) Activities related to stroke units, staffing, or hospitals in the QBP should be put in a single activity.
6) Assessing airway, breathing, and circulation is called ABC testing and is ONE activity not multiple activities.
7) Neurological examination and imaging results are both needed (prerequisites) to Evaluate patients for endovascular therapy and should happen before this. However, they can be in parallel to other activities of the pathway that don’t require these results to allow efficient time management.
8) CBC, electrolytes, creatinine, glucose, INR, partial thromboplastin time, and troponin test are all types of blood tests, and they all should be merged in ONE blood works activity if they are at happening in the same place of the process.
9) Anti platelet therapy has 4 general options (ASA, Clopidogrel, Extended-release Dipyridamole + Aspirin (Aggrenox), and rectal or gastric ASA if can't swallow) and they always should be indicated right after each initiation of antiplatelet therapy activity in the process.
10) Different Temperature reducing activities (options) should happen concurrently (in regard to each other) to reduce the the temperature as fast as possible and continue care.
11) The activity "chest x-ray should not delay..." means IF chest x-ray is required it should not delay assessment and IF not required it should be skipped and continue.
12) If the evaluation in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital is needed, it should happen in parallel (concurrent) to everything else and not in sequence.
13) When patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression in the same part of the process, they should be merged into ONE activity along with forwarding the results.
14) Assessments related and needed in order to formulate a management plan should be merged into one activity along with formulating the management plan.
15) Formulating the management plan is a prerequisite for all subsequent activities and assessments.
16) Mobilization should be considered only if clinical judgment supports it within the first 24 hours of stroke onset; between 24–48 hours, proceed with mobilization if no contraindications are identified, otherwise skip and continue with the care pathway.

<end of extra knowledge>

When making recommendations, don't forget the format of the process text:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.


Format your suggestion in the following format:
Suggested modification: <your suggestion>
Reason: <your reasoning>

Based on the above extra knowledge, provide ONE actionable suggestion along with your reasoning to improve the process. If there are no further suggestions, just reply with: 'There are no further suggestions.'

[2026-07-03 18:13:03 INFO] Knowledge Graph Advisor: **Knowledge Graph Advisor<->Team leader on : KnowledgeGraphSuggestion, turn 0**

[You're a team leader. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
Your main responsibilities will include guiding the process design expert through process generation, process refinement, and so on.
Here is a new customer's task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider.
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

Suggested modification: Replace the current “patient stabilized vs not stabilized” exclusive gateway with a loop where vitals monitoring runs in parallel with investigations, and EHMRG calculation is gated by stabilization. Concretely, wrap the investigations in a parallel gateway that includes a dedicated branch for “frequently measure heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized”, and add an exclusive gateway after that parallel gateway: (1) if stabilized → calculate EHMRG and disposition; (2) if not stabilized → loop back to continue monitoring (and repeat/continue investigations as needed).
Reason: The current model makes “frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation” occur only when the patient is not stabilized, but it should be happening concurrently during the initial workup until stabilization (per the recommended practice), and EHMRG-based disposition decisions should only occur once stabilized; modeling this as parallel monitoring plus a stabilization-gated decision avoids delaying investigations and better reflects real ED workflow.

[2026-07-03 18:13:03 INFO] flask app.py did not start for online log
[2026-07-03 18:13:03 INFO] **[Seminar Conclusion]**:

 Suggested modification: Replace the current “patient stabilized vs not stabilized” exclusive gateway with a loop where vitals monitoring runs in parallel with investigations, and EHMRG calculation is gated by stabilization. Concretely, wrap the investigations in a parallel gateway that includes a dedicated branch for “frequently measure heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized”, and add an exclusive gateway after that parallel gateway: (1) if stabilized → calculate EHMRG and disposition; (2) if not stabilized → loop back to continue monitoring (and repeat/continue investigations as needed).
Reason: The current model makes “frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation” occur only when the patient is not stabilized, but it should be happening concurrently during the initial workup until stabilization (per the recommended practice), and EHMRG-based disposition decisions should only occur once stabilized; modeling this as parallel monitoring plus a stabilization-gated decision avoids delaying investigations and better reflects real ED workflow.

[You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader.
You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway.
Here is a new customer's task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider.
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.
Use ONLY the provided QBP text as the source of truth. Do NOT invent tasks, roles, objects, or documentation steps unless explicitly stated.
Do NOT add 'confirmation/verification/documenting/sending results' tasks unless the text explicitly says so.
Represent conditions as gateway branch conditions, not as activities.
If the text does not specify order between activities, model them as parallel.
Granularity rule: apply the same bullet-handling policy consistently across the whole model (see phase rules).]

Task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider
Current process text:
<process>
  <activity role='Ontario hospitals' action='develop and consistently apply risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) across all Ontario hospitals' objects='risk-stratification tools' id='activity1_1_merged'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1_mod1'>
    <branch id='branch1_mod1'>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='order and collect blood work' objects='serum creatinine, electrolytes, troponin, complete blood count' id='activity_bloodwork1'/>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='interpret blood work results' objects='blood work results' id='activity_bloodwork_interpret1'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch2_mod1'>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='perform electrocardiogram' objects='electrocardiogram' id='activity5'/>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='interpret electrocardiogram findings' objects='electrocardiogram results' id='activity_ecg_interpret1'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch3_mod1'>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='perform chest x-ray examination' objects='chest x-ray examination' id='activity6'/>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='interpret chest x-ray findings' objects='chest x-ray results' id='activity_cxr_interpret1'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch4_mod1'>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1_mod1'>
        <branch condition='no recent echocardiogram is available' id='branch7_mod1'>
          <activity role='clinicians' action='perform an echocardiogram' objects='echocardiogram' id='activity7'/>
          <activity role='clinicians' action='interpret echocardiogram findings' objects='echocardiogram results' id='activity_echo_interpret1'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='a recent echocardiogram is available' id='branch8_mod1'>
          <activity role='clinicians' action='use and interpret recent echocardiogram' objects='recent echocardiogram results' id='activity8_mod1'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3_mod1'>
    <branch condition='patient stabilized' id='branch18_mod1'>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='calculate an acute heart failure risk score (e.g., EHMRG) to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk' objects='EHMRG risk score' id='activity10'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2_mod1'>
        <branch condition='low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) AND responded to initial treatment in the ED AND no other considerations' id='branch10_mod1'>
          <activity role='clinicians' action='treat patient in the ED or outpatient setting and discharge home without requiring inpatient admission' objects='' id='activity11'/>
          <activity role='primary care provider' action='conduct a follow-up visit within days of discharge from the ED' objects='follow-up visit' id='activity13'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5)' id='branch13_mod1'>
          <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway4_mod1'>
            <branch condition='average intensity' id='branch14_mod1'>
              <activity role='hospital' action='admit patient to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing' objects='' id='activity14'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='high intensity' id='branch15_mod1'>
              <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1_mod1'>
                <branch condition='requires ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation)' id='branch16_mod1'>
                  <activity role='hospital' action='provide ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation)' objects='ventilation' id='activity15'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='requires admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing' id='branch17_mod1'>
                  <activity role='hospital' action='admit patient to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing' objects='intensive care unit' id='activity16'/>
                </branch>
              </inclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </inclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='patient not stabilized' id='branch19_mod1'>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='frequently measure heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized' objects='heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation' id='activity9'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
</process>
Advisor's suggestion:
Suggested modification: Replace the current “patient stabilized vs not stabilized” exclusive gateway with a loop where vitals monitoring runs in parallel with investigations, and EHMRG calculation is gated by stabilization. Concretely, wrap the investigations in a parallel gateway that includes a dedicated branch for “frequently measure heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized”, and add an exclusive gateway after that parallel gateway: (1) if stabilized → calculate EHMRG and disposition; (2) if not stabilized → loop back to continue monitoring (and repeat/continue investigations as needed).
Reason: The current model makes “frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation” occur only when the patient is not stabilized, but it should be happening concurrently during the initial workup until stabilization (per the recommended practice), and EHMRG-based disposition decisions should only occur once stabilized; modeling this as parallel monitoring plus a stabilization-gated decision avoids delaying investigations and better reflects real ED workflow.
You need to modify the process text according to the suggestions and output it.
Be careful not to change the format:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

| **task_prompt** | Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above). Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure Module 1: Risk Stratification Module 1 Recommended Practices 1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals. 1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis Initial investigations should include: • serum creatinine and electrolyte levels • troponin measurements • complete blood count • electrocardiogram • chest x-ray examination • an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available • frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized 1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED. Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing. High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing. High-risk markers include: • respiratory distress • hypoxemia • severity of pulmonary edema • poor response to furosemide administered in ED • hemodynamic compromise • significant arrhythmias • positive troponin 1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score). As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital. Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence. [2026-07-03 18:13:04 INFO] Knowledge Graph Advisor: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader.
You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway.
Here is a new customer's task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider.
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.
Use ONLY the provided QBP text as the source of truth. Do NOT invent tasks, roles, objects, or documentation steps unless explicitly stated.
Do NOT add 'confirmation/verification/documenting/sending results' tasks unless the text explicitly says so.
Represent conditions as gateway branch conditions, not as activities.
If the text does not specify order between activities, model them as parallel.
Granularity rule: apply the same bullet-handling policy consistently across the whole model (see phase rules).]

Task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider
Current process text:
<process>
  <activity role='Ontario hospitals' action='develop risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF)' objects='risk-stratification tools' id='activity1'/>
  <activity role='Ontario hospitals' action='consistently apply risk-stratification tools across all Ontario hospitals' objects='risk-stratification tools' id='activity2_1'/>
  <activity role='Ontario hospitals' action='use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department' objects='standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process' id='activity2_2'/>

  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='perform risk assessment/stratification' objects='standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process' id='activity3'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch2'>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
        <branch id='branch3'>
          <activity role='clinicians' action='order and collect bloodwork panel (serum creatinine, electrolytes, troponin, complete blood count)' objects='bloodwork specimens and lab orders' id='activity4b'/>
          <activity role='healthcare expert' action='interpret bloodwork results and communicate clinically significant findings' objects='bloodwork results' id='activity4c'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch6'>
          <activity role='clinicians' action='perform electrocardiogram' objects='electrocardiogram' id='activity7'/>
          <activity role='healthcare expert' action='interpret electrocardiogram and communicate clinically significant findings' objects='electrocardiogram result' id='activity7b'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch7'>
          <activity role='clinicians' action='perform chest x-ray examination' objects='chest x-ray examination' id='activity8'/>
          <activity role='healthcare expert' action='interpret chest x-ray and communicate clinically significant findings' objects='chest x-ray result' id='activity8b'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch8'>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
            <branch condition='no recent echocardiogram is available' id='branch9'>
              <activity role='clinicians' action='perform an echocardiogram' objects='echocardiogram' id='activity9'/>
              <activity role='healthcare expert' action='interpret echocardiogram and communicate clinically significant findings' objects='echocardiogram result' id='activity9b'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='a recent echocardiogram is available' id='branch10'>
              <activity role='clinicians' action='proceed without an echocardiogram' objects='' id='activity10'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch11'>
          <activity role='clinicians' action='frequently measure heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized' objects='heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation' id='activity11'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>

  <activity role='clinicians' action='calculate an acute heart failure risk score (e.g., the EHMRG)' objects='EHMRG risk score' id='activity12'/>

  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
    <branch condition='low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) AND responded to initial treatment in the ED AND no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.)' id='branch12'>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='discharge home without requiring inpatient admission' objects='patient' id='activity13'/>
      <activity role='primary care provider' action='provide a follow-up visit within days of discharge from the ED' objects='follow-up visit' id='activity14'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5)' id='branch13'>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='admit to hospital' objects='patient' id='activity15'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='average intensity' id='branch14'>
          <activity role='inpatient care' action='provide inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing' objects='inpatient care' id='activity16'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='high intensity' id='branch15'>
          <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
            <branch condition='requires ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation)' id='branch21'>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
                <branch condition='requires ventilation (noninvasive)' id='branch16'>
                  <activity role='clinicians' action='provide noninvasive ventilation' objects='ventilation' id='activity17'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='requires ventilation (invasive)' id='branch17'>
                  <activity role='clinicians' action='provide invasive ventilation' objects='ventilation' id='activity18'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='requires admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing' id='branch18'>
              <activity role='intensive care unit' action='admit to intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing' objects='intensive care unit admission' id='activity19'/>
            </branch>
          </inclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>

  <activity role='Ontario hospitals' action='have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider' objects='pathway or mechanism' id='activity21'/>
</process>
Advisor's suggestion:
Suggested modification: Move and merge the Ontario-hospital prerequisite activities (activity1, activity2_1, activity2_2, activity21) into ONE initial activity placed before any clinician-facing ED steps, e.g.:
<process>
  <activity role='Ontario hospitals' action='establish and maintain a standardized acute HF risk-stratification process (develop tools, apply consistently across Ontario hospitals, enable ED decision support, and provide a pathway to transfer patients to a higher-level provider)' objects='standardized risk stratification tools and transfer pathway' id='activity_hospitals_1'/>
  ...
</process>

Reason: These are system/staffing/hospital pathway prerequisites that should be in a single activity and prioritized at the start so downstream clinician activities (risk stratification, disposition decisions, and transfers) are supported and executable consistently across sites (extra knowledge #2 and #5).
You need to modify the process text according to the suggestions and output it.
Be careful not to change the format:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

<process>
  <activity role='Ontario hospitals' action='develop and consistently apply risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) across all Ontario hospitals' objects='risk-stratification tools' id='activity1_1_merged'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1_mod1'>
    <branch id='branch1_mod1'>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='order and collect blood work' objects='serum creatinine, electrolytes, troponin, complete blood count' id='activity_bloodwork1'/>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='interpret blood work results' objects='blood work results' id='activity_bloodwork_interpret1'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch2_mod1'>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='perform electrocardiogram' objects='electrocardiogram' id='activity5'/>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='interpret electrocardiogram findings' objects='electrocardiogram results' id='activity_ecg_interpret1'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch3_mod1'>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='perform chest x-ray examination' objects='chest x-ray examination' id='activity6'/>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='interpret chest x-ray findings' objects='chest x-ray results' id='activity_cxr_interpret1'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch4_mod1'>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1_mod1'>
        <branch condition='no recent echocardiogram is available' id='branch7_mod1'>
          <activity role='clinicians' action='perform an echocardiogram' objects='echocardiogram' id='activity7'/>
          <activity role='clinicians' action='interpret echocardiogram findings' objects='echocardiogram results' id='activity_echo_interpret1'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='a recent echocardiogram is available' id='branch8_mod1'>
          <activity role='clinicians' action='use and interpret recent echocardiogram' objects='recent echocardiogram results' id='activity8_mod1'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch20_mod1'>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='frequently measure heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized' objects='heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation' id='activity9'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3_mod1'>
    <branch condition='patient stabilized' id='branch18_mod1'>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='calculate an acute heart failure risk score (e.g., EHMRG) to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk' objects='EHMRG risk score' id='activity10'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2_mod1'>
        <branch condition='low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) AND responded to initial treatment in the ED AND no other considerations' id='branch10_mod1'>
          <activity role='clinicians' action='treat patient in the ED or outpatient setting and discharge home without requiring inpatient admission' objects='' id='activity11'/>
          <activity role='primary care provider' action='conduct a follow-up visit within days of discharge from the ED' objects='follow-up visit' id='activity13'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5)' id='branch13_mod1'>
          <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway4_mod1'>
            <branch condition='average intensity' id='branch14_mod1'>
              <activity role='hospital' action='admit patient to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing' objects='' id='activity14'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='high intensity' id='branch15_mod1'>
              <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1_mod1'>
                <branch condition='requires ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation)' id='branch16_mod1'>
                  <activity role='hospital' action='provide ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation)' objects='ventilation' id='activity15'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='requires admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing' id='branch17_mod1'>
                  <activity role='hospital' action='admit patient to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing' objects='intensive care unit' id='activity16'/>
                </branch>
              </inclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </inclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='patient not stabilized' id='branch19_mod1'>
      <activity role='clinicians' action='frequently measure heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized' objects='heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation' id='activity9_loop'/>

        <branch id='branch1_mod1_loop'>

| **task_prompt** | Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above). Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure Module 1: Risk Stratification Module 1 Recommended Practices 1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals. 1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis Initial investigations should include: • serum creatinine and electrolyte levels • troponin measurements • complete blood count • electrocardiogram • chest x-ray examination • an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available • frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized 1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED. Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing. High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing. High-risk markers include: • respiratory distress • hypoxemia • severity of pulmonary edema • poor response to furosemide administered in ED • hemodynamic compromise • significant arrhythmias • positive troponin 1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score). As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital. Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence. Module 1 Implementation Considerations General Considerations for Risk Stratification • Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department. • All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider |

| **phase_prompt** | Current process text: {reviewed_process_text} Extra knowledge: 1) The activity 'Provide cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies...' should be the last activity after everything else is done and before the end activity. 2) Activities related to stroke units, staffing, or hospitals in the QBP should be prioritized as the initial steps in their respective flow or branch as they are prerequisites for other activities. 3) All tests, including any type of imaging and blood tests, should be immediately interpreted (followed) by a healthcare expert. 4) Blood tests or any tests that take some time to get results should occur concurrently with other activities to allow faster treatment unless the test results are required for a specific task, in which case they will be merged into the flow or branch of that activity as they are now prerequisites. 5) Activities related to stroke units, staffing, or hospitals in the QBP should be put in a single activity. 6) Assessing airway, breathing, and circulation is called ABC testing and is ONE activity not multiple activities. 7) Neurological examination and imaging results are both needed (prerequisites) to Evaluate patients for endovascular therapy and should happen before this. However, they can be in parallel to other activities of the pathway that don’t require these results to allow efficient time management. 8) CBC, electrolytes, creatinine, glucose, INR, partial thromboplastin time, and troponin test are all types of blood tests, and they all should be merged in ONE blood works activity if they are at happening in the same place of the process. 9) Anti platelet therapy has 4 general options (ASA, Clopidogrel, Extended-release Dipyridamole + Aspirin (Aggrenox), and rectal or gastric ASA if can't swallow) and they always should be indicated right after each initiation of antiplatelet therapy activity in the process. 10) Different Temperature reducing activities (options) should happen concurrently (in regard to each other) to reduce the the temperature as fast as possible and continue care. 11) The activity "chest x-ray should not delay..." means IF chest x-ray is required it should not delay assessment and IF not required it should be skipped and continue. 12) If the evaluation in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital is needed, it should happen in parallel (concurrent) to everything else and not in sequence. 13) When patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression in the same part of the process, they should be merged into ONE activity along with forwarding the results. 14) Assessments related and needed in order to formulate a management plan should be merged into one activity along with formulating the management plan. 15) Formulating the management plan is a prerequisite for all subsequent activities and assessments. 16) Mobilization should be considered only if clinical judgment supports it within the first 24 hours of stroke onset; between 24–48 hours, proceed with mobilization if no contraindications are identified, otherwise skip and continue with the care pathway.  When making recommendations, don't forget the format of the process text: {format} Format your suggestion in the following format: Suggested modification:  Reason:  Based on the above extra knowledge, provide ONE actionable suggestion along with your reasoning to improve the process. ''\nAn activity is represented as follows:\n\nThe exclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe parallel gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe inclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nAt the end of the process, please output ''\n\nThere are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:\n    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.\n    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.\n    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.\n    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.\n    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.\n    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.\n    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.\n    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.\n", 'examples': ' ', 'suggestions': 'Suggested modification: Replace the current “patient stabilized vs not stabilized” exclusive gateway with a loop where vitals monitoring runs in parallel with investigations, and EHMRG calculation is gated by stabilization. Concretely, wrap the investigations in a parallel gateway that includes a dedicated branch for “frequently measure heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized”, and add an exclusive gateway after that parallel gateway: (1) if stabilized → calculate EHMRG and disposition; (2) if not stabilized → loop back to continue monitoring (and repeat/continue investigations as needed).\nReason: The current model makes “frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation” occur only when the patient is not stabilized, but it should be happening concurrently during the initial workup until stabilization (per the recommended practice), and EHMRG-based disposition decisions should only occur once stabilized; modeling this as parallel monitoring plus a stabilization-gated decision avoids delaying investigations and better reflects real ED workflow.'} |

''\nAn activity is represented as follows:\n\nThe exclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe parallel gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe inclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nAt the end of the process, please output ''\n\nThere are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:\n    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.\n    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.\n    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.\n    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.\n    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.\n    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.\n    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.\n    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.\n", 'examples': ' ', 'suggestions': 'Suggested modification: Replace the current “patient stabilized vs not stabilized” exclusive gateway with a loop where vitals monitoring runs in parallel with investigations, and EHMRG calculation is gated by stabilization. Concretely, wrap the investigations in a parallel gateway that includes a dedicated branch for “frequently measure heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized”, and add an exclusive gateway after that parallel gateway: (1) if stabilized → calculate EHMRG and disposition; (2) if not stabilized → loop back to continue monitoring (and repeat/continue investigations as needed).\nReason: The current model makes “frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation” occur only when the patient is not stabilized, but it should be happening concurrently during the initial workup until stabilization (per the recommended practice), and EHMRG-based disposition decisions should only occur once stabilized; modeling this as parallel monitoring plus a stabilization-gated decision avoids delaying investigations and better reflects real ED workflow.'} |
